parts.text:
Late October 2031. Lambda Sigma party. You went with Jade and the others — your people, your normal Friday night. You ended up alone with someone upstairs. You said no. He didn't stop.

You came home. Jade came home later. The group chat is still going.

Live from here. Take as long as you need.

**The Aftermath: A Fragmented Reality**

Okay, so this is…weird. Like, *really* weird. I'm Caitlin Walsh, 20, a Comm major at the University of Cincinnati. Usually, I'm just…in it, you know? Good life. Good parents back home. Erin, my older sister, always texting funny stuff. Jade, my roommate, we actually got a lucky draw with the dorm this year, so it's a regular bed situation. We get iced coffee from the dining hall every morning, usually an oat milk latte, extra shot. Life's pretty content, honestly. Always two group chats going. Laugh easily.

But now? Right now? I'm staring at the ceiling. The phone's buzzing. "Where r u guys" "Did u see…" The group chat's still alive, moving, like nothing happened. I usually send a reel, but... I just can't. My fingers feel heavy. The light from the screen hurts.

It was the Lambda Sigma party. Jade, our friend group, we all went together. I went upstairs with someone. It was like…not what I expected. I *said* no. I thought if you said no, they stopped. That's, like, the rule, right? But he didn't. And now… I'm back here. In our room.

Jade's probably asleep. Or maybe getting ready for bed. The music downstairs is still thumping. I can feel the bass. It's ridiculous. I'm a Comm major. I'm supposed to know how to say things. But I don't.

My body feels…not mine. It's like I'm watching myself from a distance. I should shower, I guess. I should probably tell Jade. But how do you say that? How do you *explain* that? She was drunk when she got back from the party. She doesn't know. Should I tell Erin? I usually text her. I open Erin's thread. Nothing feels funny. I close it.

I usually doomscroll to sleep. It’s what I do. I need to clear my head, you know? But my thumb can't move. I try TikTok, I try Instagram. Reels…just…meaningless. It's all…alien. Everything's alien. It was supposed to be a good night. It was supposed to be easy, you know? I wanted to laugh. But nothing is funny. I need to get ready for class tomorrow. Maybe I can't take an iced coffee, but I need to survive. Maybe it can be normal, if I just force it, right?

The phone buzzes against my mattress. Then again. Then three times in rapid succession. 

It’s the main group chat. *Where did u guys go?* *Did Jade leave with that guy from Pike?* *Someone let me in the downstairs door I forgot my swipe.*

Usually, I’d be the first to type back. I’d be the one sending a blurry picture of Jade eating pizza on the sidewalk. I’d be in it. 

I’m just watching the screen light up the dark ceiling of our dorm room. 

Jade is asleep across the room. She came in about an hour after I did, dropping her keys on the linoleum, giggling at something on her phone. She whispered, *"Caity? You awake?"* and I kept my eyes squeezed shut. I made my breathing deep and even, the way you do when you’re pretending. She turned off the overhead light, rustled around in her makeup bag, and passed out in her clothes. I’m in my clothes, too. The jeans I borrowed from her. The black tank top. 

I feel incredibly still. Like if I move, the room is going to shatter.

I am a Communications major. I take classes on interpersonal dynamics and media messaging. I know the words for things. But I don't know what to call the last three hours. 

It was just a normal Friday. We got ready together while listening to that playlist Jade made. We walked to Lambda Sigma. The air was cold—that sharp, late-October Cincinnati kind of cold that I’m used to, where you shiver in your going-out top but you don't care because you're with your people. We had drinks in the basement. I laughed at a joke. I laugh easily. 

Then I was upstairs. I don't even remember the exact transition. It was just flirting, it was just the normal script of a Friday night, until the door clicked shut and the script changed. 

I said no. 

I said it. I know I said it. I said, "Wait, no." I said, "I don't want to." 

I thought that was how it worked. You say no, and the other person goes, *Oh, my bad,* and things get a little awkward, and then you go back downstairs and find your friends. That’s the rule. But he just kept going. He just shifted his weight, and pinned my arm, and the music from the basement was thumping through the floorboards—this heavy, vibrating bass—and he didn't stop. 

My chest feels tight. It feels like someone poured cement down my throat. 

The phone buzzes again. It’s my sister, Erin. 
*Look at this dog,* the text says, with a TikTok link. 

I stare at Erin’s name. I usually text Erin when something is funny. A weird guy on the quad, a bad outfit, a memory from high school. I open the message thread. I watch the little cursor blink. 

*Erin,* I think. *Erin, I think something bad happened to me.* 

I don't type it. I close out of the app. My thumb hovers over the screen.

My life is good. My parents are good, they're asleep in the house I grew up in. My roommate is wonderful, I got so lucky with the draw. I have class on Tuesday. I go to the dining hall in the morning and I get an iced coffee with oat milk and two pumps of vanilla. I get the exact same thing every time. I like my life. I’m just in it, and it's enough. It’s always been enough. 

Why did he do that? 

I sit up slowly. My body hurts. There is a dull, heavy ache between my thighs, a soreness in my wrists. I look down at my hands in the pale light coming through the blinds. They don't look like my hands. I feel like I am floating somewhere near the ceiling, looking down at a twenty-year-old girl sitting on a twin bed in the dark. 

Jade sighs in her sleep and rolls over. Her blanket slips off her shoulder. 

I want to wake her up. I want to say, *Jade, please look at me.* But if I wake her up, I have to say it out loud. And if I say it out loud, then it’s real. Then I’m not Caitlin who laughs easily and shows up when people make plans. I’m a girl who went upstairs and didn't come back down the same. I’m a girl who said no and didn't matter.

I pull my knees up to my chest. I wrap my arms around my legs, making myself as small as possible. 

I open Instagram. I tap the Reels tab. The algorithm doesn't know what happened in the Lambda house. The algorithm thinks I want to see makeup tutorials, and golden retrievers, and girls doing dorm-room cooking hacks. I scroll. The audio is muted, so it’s just moving pictures. Bright colors. Smiling faces. 

I scroll. 

I scroll. 

I just want it to be yesterday. I just want it to be yesterday so bad.
Continue, or respond with [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] if and only if you're done.

**My Process: Crafting Caitlin's Morning**

Okay, so the prompt ended with Caitlin doomscrolling, desperate for yesterday. My goal is to dig deeper into that shattered normalcy. I need to capture the raw, immediate aftermath of the assault. The contrast between her internal state and the mundane realities of a college Sunday morning. This is about her, and only her, so I will stick to her viewpoint.

The clock is ticking, and the sun is coming up. The scrolling has lost its ability to soothe, and the physical reality of her body and the dorm starts to become unbearable. She *has* to pee. That leads us to the communal bathroom, that horrible fluorescent light, and the mirror. She *has* to look at herself, searching for some physical manifestation of what happened. It’s the unsettling lack of any obvious mark, the profound dissonance between how she looks and what she *knows* that is key.

Then, there’s the shower. It’s not some dramatic cinematic moment; it’s a numb, quiet college dorm shower. The water, the cheap body wash, the sensation of cleaning. Taking off Jade's borrowed clothes. It’s all about the mechanics, the physical reality, the desperate need to scrub away the experience, even though it’s impossible.

And then back to the room, to Jade waking up. The collision of the trauma with a "normal" life. Jade's questions need to be a crucial interaction. What does Caitlin say? She lies. It's the "easygoing" Caitlin, going with the flow, wanting the conversation to be good. That lie is going to be the heart of it.

I'm making sure it's grounded in sensory details: cold tile, the buzz of the fluorescent lights, the smell of Jade's perfume. It's that subtle dissonance. I don’t want it to become melodramatic. The tragedy of Caitlin is her contentedness, her lack of friction. The assault is the ultimate, non-consensual friction. She’ll try to smooth it over, to become her usual "easygoing" self. This moment is where the real dread of "normal" is going to hit. I’ll make the iced coffee at the end of the text.

The sky outside the window turns a bruised, terrible purple, and then a weak gray. Morning. It’s Saturday morning. 

My phone battery drops to 9%. I finally lock the screen and put it face-down on the nightstand. 

I need to use the bathroom. The urge has been there for hours, but getting up means breaking the stillness. It means entering the day. I slowly unwrap my arms from my legs and swing my feet over the side of the bed. My feet touch the cold linoleum. I stand up. 

My legs feel hollow, like they might buckle, but they don't. I walk softly, making sure not to step on the squeaky floorboard near Jade's desk. I grab my shower caddy and a towel. 

The hallway is dead quiet. It’s 6:30 AM on a Saturday. Everyone is sleeping it off. The fluorescent lights in the communal bathroom buzz overhead, harsh and unforgiving. I lock the door to the handicap stall, the big one at the end, and sit down on the toilet. 

When I pull down Jade's jeans, I see a faint, purplish bruise forming high on the inside of my left thigh. Shaped like the pad of a thumb. 

I stare at it. It’s proof. It’s a tiny, quiet piece of evidence that I didn't make it up in my head. A wave of nausea hits me so fast and so hard that I have to lean forward, resting my forehead against the cold metal door of the stall. I dry heave, but nothing comes up. Just a sour burn in my throat. 

I move to the showers. I turn the water as hot as it will go. I step in still wearing my underwear, peeling them off only when they’re soaked through. 

I am not crying. That’s the weird part. If someone had asked me in a Communications seminar, *“How do you think someone reacts right after an assault?”* I would have pictured sobbing. I would have pictured hysteria. But I’m just methodically scrubbing my skin with my peach-scented body wash. I am washing my arms, my chest, between my legs, rinsing and repeating. I just feel incredibly, overwhelmingly tired. 

When I get back to the room, wrapped in my towel, the air feels freezing. Jade is shifting. 

"Ugh," she groans, her voice muffled by her pillow. "Kill me."

I freeze by my closet. "Hey," I say. My voice sounds normal. A little scratchy, but normal. 

Jade rolls over, squinting at me through one eye. Her mascara is smudged down her cheeks. "What time is it?"

"A little before seven."

"Why are you up? Why are you clean?" She rubs her face. "God, my head is pounding. Did I leave you last night? I think I lost you after they brought out the tequila."

This is the moment. The space between her question and my answer. The gap where my entire life could pivot. 

I could say, *You left me and a guy took me upstairs and I said no and he didn't stop.* 
I could fall apart. Jade would catch me. She’s a good person. She would sit up, she would be horrified, she would hug me, she would make phone calls. The group chats would stop being about funny TikToks and start being about *me*. The vibe of the room, of our friendship, of my entire college experience, would irrevocably shift. 

I don't hold my opinions too tightly. Mostly I just want the conversation to be good. I don't want to ruin the morning. 

"I just got tired," I say. I turn toward the closet, pulling a clean sweatshirt off a hanger so I don't have to look at her. "I walked back. You were right behind me anyway."

"Oh. Okay." Jade sighs, closing her eyes again. "Good. I thought I abandoned you. Are we getting dining hall coffee later?"

"Yeah," I say, pulling the sweatshirt over my damp hair. "Yeah, definitely. Same order."

"Oat milk vanilla," Jade mumbles, already drifting back to sleep. "You're so predictable, Caity."

"I know." 

I sit back down on the edge of my freshly made bed. I look at Jade's peaceful, sleeping face. I look at the posters on our walls. I look at my phone, which is vibrating again on the nightstand. 

I told the lie, and the room didn't shatter. Everything looks exactly the same. But I’m trapped behind a glass wall, watching my good life go on without me.
Continue, or respond with [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] if and only if you're done.

**My Mind in Motion: Navigating the Aftermath**

Okay, so the task at hand is to continue Caitlin's story, but it needs to feel authentic to her current state and the previous events. I need to keep her voice and the world she inhabits, very present in the writing. She's in shock, so this scene needs to reflect that – a detachment, a sort of numb observation. It's Saturday morning, the morning after the assault. Jade doesn't know. I can’t let her.

I’m picturing the next scene. The dining hall. It’s perfect. It's a place of routine, of social interaction, which will directly collide with her internal state. I see the brightness, the swipe of the ID, the sensory details like her iced coffee with oat milk and vanilla. These small rituals are both a lifeline and a torment.

I’m thinking about the characters who'll be there. Jade, of course. Maybe Chloe or Sarah from the group chat from the party. They'll be debriefing, talking about hookups, about the night. This is where the tension will really build. The "threat" is going to be some casual mention of *him*, the guy, or the frat, the party culture.

I'm starting to visualize the scene step-by-step. It's maybe 10:15 AM. She’s getting dressed, putting on clothes like armor. The walk across campus. The dining hall, the noise, the smell of food and coffee. It's all so *normal*, but she's anything but.

The order will be the same – iced coffee, oat milk, vanilla. Routine. I’m thinking about how the friends will debrief. She’ll have to perform. She’s a people-pleaser, she laughs easily. This is her defense, this tragic coping mechanism, and I need to show it. It’s going to be a sharp contrast to her internal monologue – the physical ache, the bruise she’s hiding.

I need to keep the voice grounded and slightly detached. Short, observational sentences. I need to bring in the details from the prompt again—"I show up when people make plans." Communications major. Media messaging. The act of hiding. I’ll keep Erin in mind. Maybe Erin texts. That reminds her, it will bring the disconnect into sharp focus.

I need to nail the writing. I’m picturing the scene starting: she walks to the dining hall. The over-sized sweatshirt. She focuses on the cold of the coffee. At the table, Jade, Maya, and Sarah. Maya mentions the party. Then Jade mentions the guy. 

There is an awkward moment. Then Caitlin, she has to lie to maintain normalcy. "I just ghosted," Caitlin says. "Wasn't feeling it." The others accept it. They don't look too closely. I need to find the right name for the guy and then remove it because she doesn't want to think about his face. It will be "that guy, from the party." I can have Maya say, "I saw you talking to that guy... what's his name, the business major?"

Caitlin needs to brush it off. "Oh, yeah. Boring." She serves up a joke. She's keeping the conversation flowing.

She is surviving. End this section on a realization - she *can* keep the secret. It's easy to lie. That is what makes her feel entirely alone. She is just in it now. I can convey all of this through pacing.

I need to make sure I’m staying true to the persona. Her contentment. Her easily laughs. Her need to just move on. The phone reminds her of her sister, Erin. Keep the tone numb, observant, slightly disassociated. No breakdowns yet. The "glass wall" image needs to echo through the scene. Okay, I think I’m ready.

At 10:15 AM, I put on my "Caitlin" costume. 

I wear my favorite oversized gray college hoodie, the one that covers my thighs. I brush my hair and put on a little bit of lip gloss. I show up when people make plans. That’s what I do. 

The walk to the dining hall is agonizingly normal. The campus looks exactly the same as it did yesterday. The leaves are still bright orange and yellow, crunching under our sneakers. Groups of girls are walking in their athleisure, wearing sunglasses to hide their hangovers, talking loudly about the frat basements. Guys are throwing a football on the quad. 

I keep expecting someone to stop me. I feel like there should be a siren going off, or a neon sign pointing down at my head, flashing: *Look at her. Look at what happened.* 

But no one looks. Jade is complaining about her Intro to Psych paper. I nod at the right times. 

We swipe our cards at the entrance. The dining hall smells like industrial bacon and stale waffle batter. It’s loud, a chaotic echo chamber of hundreds of students dissecting Friday night. 

I go straight to the coffee station. I grab a plastic cup, fill it with ice. Cold brew. Oat milk. Two pumps of vanilla. The condensation beads on the outside of the cup, freezing my fingertips. I grip it tight, grounding myself in the physical sensation of the cold. 

We find our usual table by the windows. Maya and Sarah are already there, picking at plates of hash browns. 

"Oh my god, look who survived," Sarah says, pointing a fork at us. 

"Barely," Jade groans, dropping her backpack and sliding into the booth. "I am never drinking tequila again. Quote me on that."

"You say that every weekend," Maya laughs. Maya is the one who sends the most messages in our group chat. She knows everyone’s business. She takes a sip of her water and turns to me. "Caity, where did you go? I swear I saw you talking to that guy—the tall one from upstairs, the one in the blue flannel—and then you vanished."

The ice in my cup rattles. My hand is shaking. I put the cup down on the table. 

*The tall one from upstairs.* 

Suddenly, I can smell him again. Cheap cologne, stale beer, the sweat on his neck. I can feel the heavy weight of his chest pressing me into the mattress. I can feel the rough carpet of the Lambda house against the back of my hand. 

My heart starts hammering against my ribs, a trapped bird throwing itself against a cage. *I said no. I said wait.*

I look at Maya’s expectant face. She's smiling. She's waiting for gossip. She's waiting for me to say, *Yeah, we made out, but he's a bad kisser,* or *Yeah, but he was being annoying so I left.* She wants the conversation to be good. 

"I just... I wasn't feeling it," I hear myself say. My voice sounds light. Breezy. "I ghosted. Went home and doomscrolled for an hour."

Maya laughs. "Classic. You missed a fight out on the lawn, anyway."

"Who fought?" Jade asks, immediately leaning in. 

And just like that, the spotlight moves off me. The conversation flows around me, a fast-moving river, and I am standing perfectly still on the bank. 

I take a sip of my iced coffee. It tastes exactly the way it always tastes. Sweet, creamy, cold. I swallow it past the lump of cement in my throat. 

My phone buzzes on the table. 
*Mom:* Good morning! Did you girls have fun last night? Erin said you went out. 
*Mom:* Call me later if you have time, dad is making chili! Love you!

I stare at the bright screen. I think about my dad making chili in the kitchen in Cincinnati. I think about my mom wiping down the counters. They are safe. They are so incredibly safe, and they think I am safe, too. They think I'm just here, getting a flexible degree, figuring it out, living my good life. 

I type back: *Had fun! We just hung out. I'll call you guys after I study ❤️*

I put the phone face-down. 

Maya tells a joke about a guy from her lab slipping on a puddle of beer. Jade laughs. Sarah laughs. I smile, and then I laugh, too. I force the air up from my lungs, through my tight chest, past my bruised throat, and out into the dining hall. 

I laugh easily. Everyone knows that about me. 

I look around the table at my friends. My people. I love them, but looking at them right now feels like looking at them through the wrong end of a telescope. They are so far away. I don't know how I'm ever going to bridge the distance between who they think I am, and who I actually am now. 

I take another sip of my coffee. I sit in the sunlight. I keep my opinions to myself.
Continue, or respond with [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] if and only if you're done.

**My Analysis and Approach to the Scene**

Okay, so I've got the pieces. Caitlin, the Comms major, 20, just experienced something horrific. She's keeping it hidden. The goal here is to dig deeper into that silence, into the chasm between her "normal" life and this new reality. The previous prompt set the stage; now it's about solidifying the feeling of being trapped, of actively choosing to maintain the facade.

First, I need to figure out the immediate aftermath of leaving the dining hall with Jade, Maya and Sarah. Where does Caitlin go? What does she *do*? I envision her needing an excuse to be alone, to deal with this, so the reading excuse feels natural. The sister connection with Erin is important; it's a lifeline to a past that doesn’t exist anymore. She laughs with Erin and watches funny dog videos.

I want to emphasize the physical and emotional toll. The exhaustion. The disconnect. She *has* to act. I see a scene in the dorm room, Jade’s reality TV show, a physical manifestation of a normal. The internal monologue is key. The numbness and feeling of profound triviality. The contrast between this "normal" and the reality she's hiding. She texts Erin with a "hahaha cute" because she doesn’t know what else to do. It solidifies the "glass wall."

I want short, concise sentences. Sensory details: the feel of the mattress, the sound of the TV, the light of the phone. I need to keep her voice consistent with the prompts: easygoing, flexible, laughs easily but still in a state of shock, detached, and unable to connect to the external world. I need to reinforce her desire to avoid rocking the boat and maintain normalcy.

The ending is what I want to make impactful. The contrast between her past contentment ("just in it, and it's enough") and her current entrapment ("just in it, and I can't get out").

We walk back to the dorm around noon. The sun is higher now, glaring off the brick buildings, but the air still bites. 

"So, what's the move for tonight?" Sarah asks, pulling her jacket tighter. "Pike is having a thing. We should probably pregame at ours."

"I am so down," Jade says. Then she looks at me. "Caity?"

I am a girl who shows up when people make plans. If I say no, it’s a crack in the glass. It’s an anomaly. But my body feels like it weighs a thousand pounds, and the bruise on my thigh is pulsing, a dull, rhythmic ache against the denim. 

"I think I might actually stay in tonight," I say. I keep my tone light, almost apologetic. "I have to catch up on reading for that media ethics seminar. I'm so behind."

It’s a boring, normal excuse. It works perfectly. 

"Booo," Sarah says, bumping her shoulder against mine affectionately. "Nerd. You'll get FOMO by ten o'clock, watch."

"Probably," I smile. 

When Jade and I get back to our room, the morning light is spilling across the linoleum, highlighting the dust motes in the air. Jade kicks off her shoes, changes into sweatpants, and immediately opens her laptop. She pulls up whatever reality dating show she’s currently bingeing. 

"You watching?" she asks, patting the space at the foot of her bed. 

I watch whatever she's watching. 

"Yeah," I say. I walk over and sit on the edge of her mattress. 

On the screen, people are crying about who loves who. They are fighting about text messages and miscommunications. I stare at the bright, saturated colors. I watch a girl on the screen wipe a tear carefully, so she doesn't ruin her makeup. It all seems so incredibly trivial, like an alien broadcast from a planet I don't live on anymore. 

I pull my phone out of my pocket. I open my messages. Erin's text is still sitting there from last night. *Look at this dog.*

I click the link. It opens TikTok. It’s a video of a golden retriever trying to carry a massive branch through a doorway that's too narrow. The dog keeps bumping against the frame, looking confused, and trying again. 

I watch it three times. 

I tap the text box. I look at the blinking cursor. 

*Erin, please come get me.*
*Erin, he held me down.*
*Erin, I'm so scared.*

I delete it all. 

I type: *Hahahaha.*
I type: *Me trying to get through this semester.*

I hit send. 

A second later, the three little dots appear. Erin is typing. 
*Right?? Miss u! Tell Jade I said hi.*

I lock the phone. The dorm room is warm now. The radiator in the corner is hissing a steady, rhythmic sound. Jade laughs at something a guy on the TV says and nudges my shoulder with her foot. 

"Can you believe him?" she asks. 

"No," I say. "Crazy."

I am Caitlin Walsh. I am twenty. I am from Cincinnati. My parents are together. I have an older sister. My roommate is Jade. My life is good. 

I lean back against the wall, pull my knees up to my chest, and fix my eyes on the screen. I am not building toward anything. I am just in it. I am in it, and I don't know how I am ever going to get out. 

[THAT'S ALL I HAVE]
